meta.title: Drift — Sculptural Sound Art
meta.description: Drift reimagines the white noise machine as sculptural sound art, examining the history of sonic self-control from Buckwalter's first noise machine to the present.

header.title: Drift
header.subtitle: Sculptural sound art between noise and music.

hero.title: The white noise machine, reimagined as kinetic sound sculpture.
hero.introCopy: Scaled larger-than-human, Drift reimagines the white noise machine as an object you actively listen to rather than ignore. Each sculpture — built from wood, tuned brass tubes, servo motors, and an Arduino microcontroller — slowly shifts from noise toward indeterminate musical patterns.

noteCards.title: An interdisciplinary collaboration
noteCards.body: The project team designed the sound, programming, structure, and fabrication as an integrated installation.

noteCards.title: The central question
noteCards.body: What do we lose when we control our soundscape?

theory.title: How noise, sound, and listening shape daily life.
theory.description: Technologies like white noise machines modify perception and reshape mood and attention. These are what Hagood calls orphic media. From Buckwalter's first white noise machine to Irv Teibel's Environments records to Bose's noise-cancelling headphones, people have spent decades engineering their own silence. Drift transforms that impulse into sculpture by making the pursuit of sonic control physical and immediate. And by scaling the work beyond what can be experienced all at once, the installation creates a space audiences inhabit rather than observe.

cards.title: Scale as confrontation
cards.body: Drift redesigns the white noise machine many times larger than its commercial counterpart, making it impractical for its intended purpose — masking unwanted sound in a living environment. You confront the noise you'd usually ignore.

cards.title: What silence reveals
cards.body: Tinnitus is sometimes described as the brain's response to silence — our attention straining to find meaning in the quiet. Many tinnitus sufferers use white noise machines to mask the ringing. The brain fills silence with noise; we cover that noise with more noise. Drift makes that loop audible.

cards.title: Noise as creative material
cards.body: The sculptures position noise not as something to mask or eliminate, but as creative material. Rather than mask or eliminate noise, the sculptures shape it.

future.title: Four sculptures
future.body: Currently, Drift consists of a single kinetic sculpture. Next, the installation will expand to four progressively smaller but identical sculptures — surrounding the listener in white noise and creating an environment they can move through.

quote.text: We spend our lives controlling what we hear through masking and curating. Drift asks what happens when we stop managing the soundscape and start listening to it.

footer.text: Drift will debut at DSU Research Week, Dakota State University, Madison, SD. The project is supported by DSU through a Faculty Research Initiative Grant.
